text.title: A11y Annotation Pattern Library Core Rules
text.summary: Five hard rules for the accessibility annotation pattern library used during design handoff.

rule.statement: Annotations are organized by ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG) archetype, not by visual style. Combobox, dialog, listbox, menu, menubar, radiogroup, tab, treeview — these 8 archetypes cover ~85% of components. A "fancy dropdown" is a combobox or a listbox — pick one and use that archetype's template.

rationale.source: W3C ARIA APG 2024; Stark research on a11y annotation adoption

rule.rationale: Designers default to invented annotation when there's no shared vocabulary. Tying templates to APG archetypes means dev recognizes the pattern instantly and there's a canonical W3C reference for ambiguous cases. Reduces dev's "what's the right role?" questions to zero.

rule.statement: Every interactive layer in the design has an annotation before the dev-handoff doc is sent. "Interactive" = anything that receives focus, hover, or click. Static text, decorative images, and pure layout containers do not need annotations.

rationale.source: Microsoft inclusive design tenets; Salesforce a11y SDK practice

rule.rationale: Annotating during design (vs. during code review) costs 5-10 min per component; retrofitting in code costs 1-3 hours per component (data: Salesforce internal). The 12-30x cost difference is the entire business case. Pre-handoff annotation prevents the "we forgot to make this keyboard-accessible" sprint surprise.

rule.statement: Every annotation has at minimum: (a) ARIA role or HTML element, (b) accessible name (what the screen reader announces), (c) all states the component can be in (default / hover / active / focused / disabled / loading / error). Missing any of the three blocks the annotation from being considered complete.
rule.rationale: SC 4.1.2 is the most-violated WCAG criterion (WebAIM Million 2024: 53% of homepages fail). Role + name + state IS 4.1.2 conformance. Forcing all three in the template makes the criterion automatic.

rule.statement: For any screen with > 3 interactive elements OR any dialog / modal / drawer, the design explicitly numbers focus order. Annotations include "tab order: 1, 2, 3 …" with arrows on the design surface. Visual reading order and tab order must match WCAG 2.4.3.

rationale.source: WCAG 2.1 SC 2.4.3 Focus Order; Deque modal pattern guide

rule.rationale: Modal focus traps are the #1 source of "keyboard user can't escape" bugs. Without explicit numbering, dev defaults to DOM order which doesn't match the visual hierarchy. Numbering forces the designer to walk the page with keyboard mentally.

rule.statement: Each annotation lists the keyboard interactions: Tab/Shift+Tab navigation, Enter/Space activation, Arrow keys for composite widgets, Escape for dismissal. Use APG's canonical key map per archetype — never invent keys ("Alt+L to open menu" is wrong unless documented as a custom shortcut with disclosure).

rationale.source: W3C ARIA APG canonical keys; jQuery UI legacy issues

rule.rationale: Custom key maps create discoverability holes. APG canonical keys are what screen reader users have already learned. The annotation is the spec — if it says "Arrow Down opens menu", dev knows exactly what to implement and what to test.

example.title: Annotation snippet for a combobox
example.code: Component: SearchAutocomplete Archetype: combobox (APG) ARIA role: combobox + listbox (composite) Accessible name: "Search products" (from visible label, not placeholder) States: collapsed / expanded / loading / no-results / error Tab order: 6 of 12 on this screen Keyboard: - Tab: focus into the input - Type: filters listbox - Arrow Down: opens listbox and moves to first option - Arrow Up/Down: navigate options - Enter: select option, close listbox - Escape: close listbox without selecting, keep focus on input Live region: aria-live="polite" announces "{n} results"
example.note: Illustrates all 5 rules: archetype-keyed, role+name+states populated, tab-order numbered, keyboard map canonical.

example.title: Generic annotation that says nothing
example.code:
Component: SearchAutocomplete Annotation: "Make sure this is accessible"
Problem: violates r1, r3, r5. Dev has no information. Falls back to whatever HTML they think works. End result: a div with click handlers, no keyboard support, no role, no name. WCAG 2.4.3, 4.1.2 violated. Repair: use the archetype snippet from the library. 5 min vs. 3 hours later.
example.note: The "be accessible" annotation is worse than no annotation — it creates the illusion of coverage.
